Title
Environment Reactivity Prompt

Purpose
Make Shadow Shift's world feel more alive by letting hazards, barriers, platforms, switches, and ambient objects respond more clearly to player abilities and world states.

Why This Matters for V2
Reactive environments make the world feel authored and intelligent. They also make abilities feel meaningful outside of combat.

Detailed Codex Instructions
Context:
The game already has some reactive objects, such as barriers and world-swapped platforms. V2 should generalize and expand this behavior.

Goal:
Build a shared environment reactivity layer that connects player actions to world responses in a readable, extensible way.

Implementation Steps:
1. Audit current reactive objects and one-off interaction logic.
2. Create a common interaction contract for world objects, including:
   - accepted triggers
   - response behavior
   - persistent versus temporary state
   - visual feedback hooks
3. Update several existing object types to use the shared model.
4. Add at least one or two richer reactive world behaviors that combine movement, Shadow Swap, or elements.
5. Preserve simple collision and room geometry.

Constraints:
- No full puzzle engine rewrite
- Keep the world readable and performant
- Preserve current level flow

Implementation Notes
- Prefer a small set of composable triggers and responses.
- Keep persistent and temporary state separate.
- Use this system to reduce hardcoded special cases.

Validation Checklist
- Existing reactive objects still work
- New reactive behaviors feel coherent
- Object logic is easier to extend
- Visual feedback remains readable
- The world feels more systemic than before
